coverage_notes: Reinforces states_of_matter from the particle perspective. No exact standalone curriculum match — this is foundational scaffolding that makes atomic_structure and bonding intuitive later.

rendering_guide.section: Headed paragraph block with spacing above/below
rendering_guide.callout: Highlighted box (colored background, icon optional). Styles: definition, key_idea, fun_fact, remember, mind_blow
rendering_guide.list: Styled list. Styles: icon_list, two_column, comparison, challenge, numbered_steps
rendering_guide.visual: Placeholder for diagram/illustration — describes what to show
rendering_guide.transition: Italic bridge text, lighter font weight

student_flow.step_1: READ Part 1 (hook — why should particles matter to me?)
student_flow.step_2: READ Part 2 (core — the particle model of matter)
student_flow.step_4: WATCH matched video (particle-focused, animated showing particle motion)
student_flow.step_5: READ Part 3 (particles explain the three states)

parts.purpose: Hook — pose a mystery that particles solve

blocks.body: Drop a sugar cube into a cup of hot tea. Watch it carefully. The cube sits at the bottom, edges start to crumble, and within a minute — it's gone. Completely invisible. But take a sip. It's sweet.
The sugar didn't vanish. It didn't teleport. It didn't turn into something else. So where did it go?

This question — where does something go when it disappears but is still clearly there — puzzled thinkers for thousands of years. The answer launched all of chemistry and changed how we understand reality: matter is made of incredibly tiny particles, far too small to see.

blocks.title: Try This Right Now
blocks.body: Make yourself a cup of tea, coffee, or just warm water. Drop in a sugar cube or a spoonful of sugar. Don't stir — just watch.
See the edges softening? The cube is shrinking. After a minute or two, it's gone. Now take a sip. Sweet. The sugar is still there — you can taste it. But you can't see a single grain.

Now try something else. Spray perfume, deodorant, or air freshener in one corner of the room. Stand in the far corner. Wait. Within a minute or two, you'll smell it — even though nobody carried it over to you and there's no wind.

Two mysteries with one answer: the sugar broke into pieces too small to see. The perfume traveled through the air without being pushed. Both happen because matter is made of particles in constant motion.
Here's a magic trick that isn't magic at all. Step 1: drop sugar into tea. Step 2: wait. Step 3: the sugar vanishes. Step 4: taste the tea. It's sweet. The sugar is somehow there AND not there.

This isn't David Copperfield. This is chemistry. And the explanation is arguably more impressive than any magic trick — everything you've ever touched, tasted, or smelled is made of particles so tiny that ten million of them in a line would barely span a millimeter.

The sugar didn't vanish. It broke into pieces so small your eyes can't see them, and those pieces scattered among the water pieces. Your tongue can still find them. Your eyes just can't keep up. Welcome to the particle idea — where everything is tiny, everything is moving, and your senses are only catching a fraction of the action.

parts.part_title: Everything Is Made of Tiny Particles
parts.purpose: Core teaching — the particle model, constant motion, diffusion

blocks.title: The Particle Model
blocks.body: All matter is made of incredibly tiny particles that are constantly moving. The type, arrangement, and motion of these particles determine every property of matter.

blocks.title: How Tiny Is Tiny?
blocks.body: These particles are unimaginably small. A single grain of sand contains about 50 quintillion particles (that's 50 followed by 18 zeros). A drop of water has more particles than there are stars in the observable universe. You will never see an individual particle with your naked eye — but the evidence for their existence is everywhere.

blocks.title: They Never Stop Moving
blocks.body: Here's the part that surprises most people: particles are ALWAYS moving. Even in a solid brick wall. Even in an ice cube sitting perfectly still in your freezer. The particles inside are vibrating, jostling, buzzing with energy. Nothing in the universe is truly still at the particle level.

blocks.title: Temperature Is Particle Speed
blocks.body: When we say something is 'hot,' what we really mean is that its particles are moving fast. When something is 'cold,' its particles are moving slowly. Temperature is just our way of measuring average particle motion.

blocks.title: Diffusion: Particles on the Move
blocks.body: Because particles are always moving, they naturally spread out from areas where they're concentrated to areas where they're not. This is called diffusion. It's why a drop of food coloring slowly spreads through a glass of still water without stirring. It's why you can smell cookies baking from another room. It's why a gas released in one corner of a room eventually fills the entire space. No wind, no stirring, no help needed — just particles bumping into each other and gradually spreading out.

blocks.title: Three Key Principles
blocks.body: 1. All matter is made of particles. 2. These particles are in constant motion. 3. Particles spread from crowded areas to empty areas (diffusion).

blocks.title: See Diffusion With Your Own Eyes
blocks.body: Fill a clear glass with still water — don't disturb it. Now carefully place a single drop of food coloring on the surface. Don't stir. Don't touch the glass. Just watch.

The drop of color will slowly sink and spread, tendrils reaching outward like ink in slow motion. Over several minutes, the entire glass gradually becomes tinted — without any help from you. You're watching billions of dye particles colliding with water particles, being knocked in random directions, slowly spreading from where there are many to where there are few.

Nobody pushed those dye particles. Nobody stirred. The spreading happened because all particles are constantly in motion, bumping into each other randomly. This is diffusion — and it's one of the strongest pieces of evidence that particles exist and move.

blocks.title: Smell the Evidence
blocks.body: Now for an invisible version. Open a jar of something strong-smelling — coffee grounds, vinegar, perfume. Hold it at arm's length. You can smell it from there. But the jar is over there and your nose is over here.

What happened? Scent particles launched off the source, collided with air particles billions of times, zigzagged randomly through the room, and some happened to reach your nose. You just detected matter traveling through the air using nothing but your nose as the sensor.

blocks.title: Feel Temperature as Particle Speed
blocks.body: Hold an ice cube in one hand and press your other palm flat against a warm surface. Your cold hand is touching slow-moving particles. Your warm hand is touching fast-moving particles. That difference in speed IS the difference in temperature. When the ice melts in your hand, your fast-moving skin particles are transferring their energy to the slow-moving ice particles, speeding them up until they move fast enough to become liquid.

blocks.title: The Particle Model
blocks.body: Everything — literally everything — is made of teeny tiny particles having the time of their lives. They never stop moving. Even when you think something is perfectly still, its particles are throwing a microscopic rave.

blocks.title: How Small Are We Talking?
blocks.body: Small. Absurdly small. A single grain of sand has about 50,000,000,000,000,000,000 particles in it. That number is so big that if you counted one particle per second, you'd need about 1.6 trillion years. The universe is only 13.8 billion years old. You'd run out of universe before you ran out of sand particles.

blocks.title: They. Never. Stop. Moving.
blocks.body: Even in a rock. Even in an ice cube. Even in your desk that's been sitting there doing nothing since the day you bought it. Inside, the particles are vibrating like tiny overcaffeinated atoms having a constant existential crisis.

blocks.title: Temperature Is Just a Vibe Check
blocks.body: When something is 'hot,' its particles are moving fast. When it's 'cold,' they're moving slowly. Temperature is literally just us measuring how hyper the particles are. A thermometer is a vibe checker.

blocks.title: Diffusion: The Lazy Spread
blocks.body: Because particles are always bumping around randomly, they naturally spread out over time. This is called diffusion, and it explains so much. Why does food coloring spread in water without stirring? Diffusion. Why can you smell popcorn from three rooms away? Diffusion. Why does your dad's cologne arrive ten minutes before he does? AGGRESSIVE diffusion. Nobody's pushing these particles. They're just bouncing around like a crowd leaving a concert — bumping, shuffling, slowly filling every available space.

blocks.title: The Three Laws of Tiny Things
blocks.body: 1. All matter = tiny particles. 2. Particles never stop moving (even in solids). 3. Particles spread out on their own (diffusion).

questions.question: A drop of red food coloring is placed in a glass of still water. Over several minutes, the color spreads throughout the glass without stirring. Why?

options.A: The food coloring chemically reacts with water to create new colored water
options.B: Gravity pulls the color evenly downward through the glass
options.C: Dye particles and water particles are constantly moving and colliding, causing the dye to gradually spread (diffusion)
options.D: Light passing through the glass makes it look like the color spread

questions.explanation: Diffusion: particles are in constant random motion, colliding and bouncing off each other. Dye particles gradually move from where they're concentrated (the drop) to where they're not (the rest of the glass). No stirring needed.

questions.question: An ice cube sits motionless in a freezer. Are its particles moving?

options.A: No — particles in solids are completely frozen and still
options.B: Yes — the particles vibrate in fixed positions even in a solid
options.C: Only if the freezer door is open and warm air reaches them
options.D: Only the particles on the surface move; inside is completely still

questions.explanation: Particles ALWAYS move, even in solids. In a solid, they vibrate in fixed positions — they don't travel around, but they never fully stop. The only temperature where motion theoretically stops is absolute zero (-273°C), which has never been fully achieved.

questions.question: You heat a metal spoon by dipping it into hot soup. At the particle level, what's happening?

options.A: Heat is a fluid that flows from the soup into the spoon
options.B: Fast-moving soup particles collide with slower spoon particles, transferring kinetic energy and speeding them up
options.C: The spoon absorbs soup particles that make it hotter
options.D: The metal chemically reacts with the hot liquid

questions.explanation: Temperature is average particle speed. The fast-moving (hot) soup particles collide with the slower (cool) metal particles at the surface. Each collision transfers some energy, gradually speeding up the metal particles — which we feel as the spoon getting warmer.
questions.tests_concept: states_of_matter — heat transfer at particle level

parts.purpose: Connect particle behavior to solid/liquid/gas properties — deepens states_of_matter understanding

blocks.title: Same Particles, Different Behavior
blocks.body: In Part 1 of this course, we learned that matter comes in three states. Now we can explain WHY each state behaves the way it does — it all comes down to how much energy the particles have and how strongly they attract each other.

items.label: Solid: Locked in Place
items.body: Particles have the least energy. They're packed tightly together, held by strong attractions to their neighbors. They can only vibrate in place — like people standing in a crowded elevator. This is why solids hold their shape, have a fixed volume, and feel rigid.

items.label: Liquid: Sliding Around
items.body: Particles have more energy than in a solid. They're still close together, but they have enough energy to break free of their fixed positions and slide past each other. Like people in a crowded dance floor — close together but constantly shifting. This is why liquids flow, take the shape of their container, but keep a fixed volume.

items.label: Gas: Total Freedom
items.body: Particles have so much energy they've completely overcome the attractions holding them together. They fly in every direction at high speeds, spreading as far apart as their container allows. Like people released from the building entirely — running in every direction. This is why gases have no fixed shape, no fixed volume, and fill any space.

blocks.title: It's All About Energy vs. Attraction
blocks.body: Every state of matter is a balance between two forces: the energy that makes particles move apart, and the attraction that pulls particles together. In solids, attraction wins. In gases, energy wins. In liquids, they're roughly balanced.

blocks.title: What State Changes Really Are
blocks.body: When you heat a solid, you're pumping energy into its particles. At some point, they vibrate so hard they break free of their fixed positions — that's melting. Keep adding energy, and eventually they move so fast they fly completely apart — that's boiling. Cooling reverses the process: particles slow down, attractions pull them closer, and they re-organize.

blocks.title: Key Connection
blocks.body: State changes don't change WHAT the particles are — they change HOW the particles move and interact. H₂O is H₂O whether it's ice, water, or steam.

blocks.title: Build a Model With Your Body
blocks.body: Try this with friends — or imagine it vividly. Stand shoulder to shoulder in a tight group with three or four people. Everyone vibrates slightly but stays in place. You can feel the people next to you. Nobody moves from their spot. That's a solid.

Now keep standing close together, but start sliding past each other — shuffling around, changing positions, but staying as a group in roughly the same area. You're still touching neighbors, but you're not locked in place anymore. That's a liquid.

Now everyone breaks apart and runs in different directions across the room. You're bouncing off walls, bumping into each other occasionally, but mostly flying solo with huge gaps between you. That's a gas.
Same people. Same 'particles.' The only thing that changed was how much energy each person had and how much they interacted with their neighbors.

blocks.title: Now Watch It Happen for Real
blocks.body: Boil a pot of water with the lid on. Watch the lid rattle. Those water molecules just gained so much energy they're flying upward as steam, slamming into the lid with enough force to lift it. You're watching particle energy overcome an actual physical weight. Now take the pot off the heat and hold the cold lid above the steam. Watch water droplets form on the lid's underside. The fast-flying gas particles hit the cold surface, lose energy, slow down, and attractions pull them back together as liquid drops. You just watched condensation at the particle level.

blocks.title: The Real Explanation for Everything
blocks.body: Remember the three states from our last lesson? Now we can explain WHY they work the way they do. And honestly, the explanation is just a vibe check on particle energy levels.

items.label: Solid = Mosh Pit (No Moving)
items.body: Particles are packed tight, vibrating in place, going absolutely nowhere. It's like being stuck in the front row of a concert — you can shake, but you're NOT leaving your spot. This is why solids are rigid. The particles literally can't go anywhere.

items.label: Liquid = House Party
items.body: Particles have enough energy to slide around each other. They're social, they're moving, they're mingling — but they're staying in the house. Nobody's leaving the building. This is why liquids flow but keep a definite volume.

items.label: Gas = Everyone Left the Building
items.body: Particles said 'peace out' and are now flying in every direction at hundreds of meters per second. Bouncing off walls, bouncing off each other, filling every available space. Zero commitment. Zero boundaries. This is why gas fills any container.

blocks.title: The Universal Truth of States
blocks.body: Every state of matter is just a battle between 'I want to move' (energy) and 'I want to stay together' (attraction). Solids: attraction wins. Gases: energy wins. Liquids: it's a tie. That's it. That's the whole explanation.

blocks.title: State Changes = Energy Plot Twists
blocks.body: Melting is when you pump so much energy into a solid that the particles break free of their formation. It's a jailbreak. Boiling is when even the liquid can't contain them and they go full gas. Cooling is the reverse — particles lose energy, slow down, and get recaptured by attractions. Freezing is the authorities restoring order. The particles didn't change who they are. They just changed their energy levels and lifestyle choices.

questions.question: Why can you compress (squeeze) a gas into a smaller container but you can't compress a liquid or solid the same way?

options.A: Gas particles are smaller than liquid or solid particles
options.B: Gases have large empty spaces between particles that can be squeezed out; liquids and solids are already packed closely
options.C: Gas particles are lighter so they can be pushed together more easily
options.D: Liquids and solids have a protective force field that prevents compression

questions.explanation: In a gas, particles are spread far apart with mostly empty space between them. Compression pushes them closer together into that empty space. In liquids and solids, particles are already nearly touching — there's almost no space left to squeeze out.

questions.question: When you heat ice, it melts into water. At the particle level, what is the heat energy actually doing?

options.A: Breaking the water molecules apart into hydrogen and oxygen
options.B: Creating new water particles that are liquid instead of solid
options.C: Making the particles vibrate so hard they break free of their fixed positions and start sliding past each other
options.D: Removing the cold particles and replacing them with warm ones

questions.explanation: The H₂O molecules stay intact — they don't break apart. The energy makes them vibrate faster until they overcome the attractions holding them in their crystal positions. Once free to slide, the solid becomes a liquid.

questions.question: A pot of water is boiling on the stove. The temperature stays at exactly 100°C even as you keep heating it. Where is all that energy going?

options.A: The thermometer is broken — it should be rising
options.B: The energy is escaping through the pot into the stove
options.C: The energy is being used to break attractions between particles, converting liquid to gas — not to raise temperature
options.D: Water can only reach 100°C no matter how much heat you add

questions.explanation: During a state change (boiling), added energy goes into breaking intermolecular attractions rather than increasing particle speed. Since temperature measures particle speed, it stays constant until all the liquid has become gas. This energy is called latent heat.

parts.purpose: Show real-world evidence for particles, introduce Brownian motion, bridge to atoms_exist

blocks.title: If Particles Are Invisible, How Do We Know They're Real?
blocks.body: You can't see particles. You can't touch a single one. So how do scientists know they exist? The same way you know wind exists — by observing what it DOES.

items.label: Dissolving
items.body: Sugar vanishes in water but sweetness remains — the sugar broke into particles too small to see.

items.label: Diffusion
items.body: Food coloring spreads in still water. Smells travel across rooms. Particles are moving on their own.

items.label: Brownian Motion
items.body: In 1827, Robert Brown watched pollen grains jiggling randomly in water under a microscope. The grains themselves weren't alive — they were being knocked around by invisible water particles bombarding them from all sides. This random jiggling was later explained by Einstein as direct proof of particle existence.

items.label: Compression of Gases
items.body: You can squeeze a gas into a smaller volume (like in a bike pump), proving there's empty space between particles. You can't significantly compress a liquid or solid — their particles are already close together.

items.label: Expansion With Heat
items.body: Railroad tracks have gaps because metal expands in summer heat. The particles move faster and push slightly farther apart. Without gaps, the rails would buckle.

blocks.body: We don't need to see particles directly to know they're real. Their effects are visible everywhere — in dissolving, diffusion, Brownian motion, compression, and thermal expansion.

Now we know matter is made of particles that are always moving. But here's the next question: if everything is made of particles, are all particles the same? Clearly not — gold particles must be different from iron particles, otherwise gold and iron would be identical. So what ARE these particles? What makes one type different from another? The answer has a name: atoms. And that's where we're going next.

blocks.title: Experiments You Can Run Right Now
blocks.body: You've already done two of these — the sugar dissolving and the food coloring spreading. Here are more ways to observe particles in action without any lab equipment.

items.label: The Balloon Test
items.hints: Blow up a balloon and tie it. Wait three days. It's slightly smaller. Gas particles are so tiny they squeezed through microscopic pores in the rubber. Matter escaped through what looked like a sealed barrier.

items.label: The Warm Spoon Test
items.hints: Put a metal spoon in hot water. Within seconds the handle is warm. Energy traveled particle-to-particle up the entire spoon. Now try the same with a wooden spoon — much slower, because wood particles transfer energy less efficiently.

items.label: The Bathroom Mirror Test
items.hints: Take a hot shower and watch the mirror fog up. Fast-moving water vapor particles hit the cold mirror surface, lose energy, slow down, and condense into tiny liquid droplets. You're watching gas particles become liquid in real time.

items.label: The Dust Beam Test
items.hints: In a dark room, let a shaft of sunlight in through a gap in the curtains. Watch dust particles dancing in the light beam — jiggling randomly, never settling. They're being knocked around by invisible air particles. This is Brownian motion happening in your bedroom.

blocks.body: Every one of these everyday observations — deflating balloons, warm spoons, foggy mirrors, dancing dust — is direct evidence that invisible particles exist and are constantly in motion.

We now know matter is made of tiny moving particles. But here's the big question: why is gold different from iron? Why is oxygen different from carbon? If everything is particles, what makes one type different from another? The answer: not all particles are the same. They're called atoms — and each type of atom creates a different element. That's next.

blocks.title: But How Do We KNOW?
blocks.body: Fair question. We've never seen a particle with our eyes. So how do we know they're real and not just a really convincing theory? Same way we know gravity exists — we can't see it, but we can see what it DOES.

blocks.title: The Evidence (aka Particles Snitching on Themselves)

items.label: Dissolving
items.body: Sugar disappears in tea but the sweetness remains. The sugar didn't learn invisibility — it broke into particles too small for your eyes. Your tongue is a better particle detector than your eyes. Let that sink in.

items.label: Smell
items.body: Your dad's cologne arrives before he does. Those scent particles are doing parkour through the air at hundreds of meters per second, bouncing off air particles like a pinball until some reach your nose.

items.label: Brownian Motion
items.body: In 1827, a guy named Robert Brown watched pollen jiggle randomly in water. He thought the pollen was alive. It wasn't. Invisible water particles were body-checking it from all directions. Einstein later proved this was the smoking gun for particle existence. Pollen: solving physics since 1827.

items.label: Balloons Deflate
items.body: A perfectly tied balloon slowly shrinks over days. Gas particles are so tiny they squeeze through the rubber like a prisoner escaping through a vent. The balloon looks sealed. The particles disagree.

blocks.body: We literally proved atoms exist because some guy stared at pollen jiggling in water and Einstein did the math. Science is sometimes gloriously weird.

So everything is tiny particles in constant motion. Cool. But if everything is particles, why does gold look different from iron? Why is oxygen a gas and carbon a solid? Not all particles are created equal, my friend. They come in about 118 different flavors called atoms. And we're about to meet them.

questions.question: A perfectly tied balloon slowly deflates over several days. What does this tell you about gas particles?

options.A: The rubber absorbed the air and converted it to solid
options.B: Gas particles are tiny enough to pass through microscopic gaps in the rubber
options.C: The knot must have been slightly loose
options.D: Air particles inside the balloon dissolved into the rubber

questions.explanation: Gas particles (especially small ones like helium) are tiny enough to squeeze through microscopic pores in rubber. The balloon looks sealed to your eyes, but at the particle scale, it has gaps large enough for gas particles to escape.

questions.question: You see dust particles dancing randomly in a beam of sunlight. What causes this motion?

options.A: Air currents from the window are blowing the dust around
options.B: The sunlight pushes the dust with light energy
options.C: Invisible air particles are constantly colliding with the dust from all sides, knocking it in random directions
options.D: Static electricity from the dust makes the particles repel each other

questions.explanation: This is Brownian motion — the dust particles are large enough to see, but the air particles hitting them are invisible. Billions of air particle collisions per second knock the dust in random directions, creating the visible 'dancing' effect.
questions.tests_concept: states_of_matter — Brownian motion as particle evidence

questions.question: Railroad tracks are built with small gaps between sections. Why?

options.A: The gaps let rainwater drain through so the tracks don't rust
options.B: It's cheaper to use shorter pieces of metal
options.C: Metal particles move faster in summer heat, causing the rails to expand slightly — gaps prevent buckling
options.D: The gaps help the train wheels grip better

questions.explanation: When metal heats up, its particles vibrate faster and push slightly farther apart — the metal expands. Railroad tracks can grow several centimeters in summer heat. Without gaps, the expanding metal would have nowhere to go and the tracks would buckle dangerously.
questions.tests_concept: states_of_matter — thermal expansion as particle evidence
